oneSentenceAnswer: React Server Components let you run React components on the server so they never ship JavaScript to the browser, making pages load faster without sacrificing interactivity.
fiveMinutePath: Understand the client–server split: what runs where.
See how RSC differs from SSR and SSG.
Learn the 'use client' and 'use server' directives.
Understand the data-fetching pattern: async components.
Know the gotchas: no useState, no event handlers in Server Components.
whyItMatters: Zero-JS Server Components shrink your bundle and improve Core Web Vitals.
Async components remove the need for useEffect-on-mount data fetching.
The mental model shift (server vs client tree) avoids a whole class of bugs.

keyIdeas.title: Server by default in Next.js 13+
keyIdeas.body: Every component in the App Router is a Server Component unless you add 'use client'. It runs once on the server and ships zero JS to the browser.

keyIdeas.title: No useState or useEffect
keyIdeas.body: Server Components cannot use React hooks that depend on the browser. If you need state, event handlers, or browser APIs, add 'use client' at the top of that file.

keyIdeas.title: Async components replace useEffect fetching
keyIdeas.body: Server Components can be async functions. Await your database or API call directly in the component body — no useEffect, no loading state, no waterfall.

keyIdeas.title: Server and Client components compose
keyIdeas.body: A Server Component can import and render a Client Component. Pass serialisable props (strings, numbers, plain objects) — not functions or class instances.

diagrams.alt: Diagram showing the Server Component tree staying on the server while Client Components (marked use client) hydrate in the browser.
diagrams.code:
graph TD
  A[Next.js App Router] --> B[Server Component]
  B --> C[Server Component]
  B --> D[Client Component\n'use client']
  C --> E[DB / API call\nasync/await]
  D --> F[useState / events\nbrowser APIs]
  style D fill:#3b1d8a,color:#fff
  style F fill:#1d3b8a,color:#fff

examples.title: Product page with zero JS for the description
examples.body: Make your ProductDescription a Server Component that fetches from a CMS directly. Only the AddToCart button needs 'use client'. The description ships no JS.

examples.title: Dashboard with async data fetching
examples.body: An async Server Component can await your database query at the top level. No useEffect, no loading spinner, no extra round-trip from the browser.

examples.title: Shared layout that reads session server-side
examples.body: A root layout Server Component can read auth session from cookies or headers directly, then pass the user name down as a prop to a client header component.

analogies.setup: Server Components are like a kitchen that preps food before service.
analogies.takeaway: The kitchen (server) does all the heavy work — chopping, cooking — and sends a finished plate. The dining room (browser) only needs a fork, not a full kitchen.

analogies.setup: Choosing 'use client' is like highlighting a section of a document that needs to be interactive.
analogies.takeaway: Everything unmarked stays static. Only the highlighted sections get JavaScript attached. Less highlighting means a lighter document.

misconceptions.myth: Server Components replace Client Components entirely.
misconceptions.truth: They complement each other. Interactive UI still needs Client Components. The goal is to keep non-interactive parts on the server to save bundle size.

misconceptions.myth: RSC is the same as SSR.
misconceptions.truth: SSR renders a snapshot and ships JS for hydration. RSC never ships its own JS — it streams a component-tree description the client runtime interprets.

glossary.definition: A React component that runs only on the server and ships no JavaScript to the browser.
A React component that runs in the browser, marked with 'use client' at the top of the file.
Attaching React event handlers to server-rendered HTML in the browser so it becomes interactive.
The Next.js 13+ routing system where every component is a Server Component by default.

practicalTakeaways: Default to Server Components; only add 'use client' when you need state or event handlers.
Fetch data directly in async Server Components instead of useEffect.
Push the 'use client' boundary as deep in the tree as possible to minimise bundle size.
Pass only serialisable values (strings, numbers, plain objects) from Server to Client Components.

sourceMap.title: React Docs — Server Components
sourceMap.summary: Official React documentation on the Server Components model, constraints, and composition rules.
claims: Hooks not allowed in Server Components

sourceMap.title: Next.js Docs — Server and Client Components
sourceMap.summary: Next.js App Router documentation covering server-first defaults, async components, and layout patterns.
claims: App Router defaults to Server Components

uncertaintyNotes: RSC is still evolving; some edge cases around streaming and Suspense boundaries change between React minor versions.
